cataloging_subject.label: An Anthology of Chancery English: selected documentary texts
cataloging_subject.scope_note: The U-M/General Corpus edition-level selection of 241 documentary texts from printed pp. 84–303 of Fisher, Richardson, and Fisher's 1984 anthology. It is not one medieval authored work, one manuscript, all contents of any cited archival object, or the complete printed volume. The source documents survive across 231 TNA citations and ten passages in seven British Library Cotton codices, with some current catalog matches only at parent-piece level.

catalog_summary.title: An Anthology of Chancery English
catalog_summary.author: Multiple anonymous and named documentary creators
catalog_summary.editor: John H. Fisher, Malcolm Richardson, and Jane L. Fisher
catalog_summary.date: Selected records currently corrected to 1384–1455 (anthology/XML headings report 1384–1462); anthology published 1984
catalog_summary.region: England, especially institutions associated with London and Westminster, with wider British and continental connections

assertion.notes: This is the edition and electronic-item title, not an assertion of organic medieval unity.

agents.name: Multiple documentary creators
notes: A cataloging collective for royal and governmental clerks, petitioners, contracting parties, and other makers represented by the selected records; it does not imply common or anonymous authorship in every item.

agents.name: Malcolm Richardson
notes: The OTA header supplies the authority form Richardson, Malcolm, 1947–.

assertion.notes: This collective responsibility covers multiple named and anonymous historical actors and avoids asserting either a single author or universal anonymity.

date_statements.display: 1384–1462 as reported by the anthology and XML headings

assertion.notes: This is a confirmed report about the edition's headings, not the corrected historical range. Benskin's manuscript collation refutes the 1462 date assigned to item 241.

date_statements.display: 1384–1455 after applying currently documented date corrections

assertion.notes: This aggregate range applies the currently documented item-level corrections, including item 234 to 1425, item 240 to 1455, and item 241 to 1436. It remains provisional until all 241 items are rechecked.
The selection belongs to English governmental and documentary culture; this does not claim every record was physically written in England.

scope.description: Numerous records concern or were associated with London, but the edition's locality labels are not reliable composition-place statements.
Numerous royal and administrative records name Westminster or institutions based there.
Embedded formulas and short passages; some accompanying Latin is mentioned but omitted by the edition.
Occasional embedded or accompanying Middle French or Anglo-French material in the documentary sources.
Modern English headings, source labels, and short editorial annotations occur across the selection.

form_statements.description: All 241 selected documentary texts are prose, though layout and documentary formulas vary.

scope.description: Edition-defined grouping; individual records may require reclassification.

subject_statements.label: History and standardization of written English

summaries.value: A modern edited selection of 241 late-fourteenth- and fifteenth-century English administrative, legal, and political documents, organized as Signet-related material, Privy Seal papers, proceedings in Parliament and Chancery, and indentures. The corpus is important for documentary practice and the history of written English, but its 1984 transcriptions, archival references, dates, localities, and departmental labels require item-level checking against current catalogs and surviving witnesses.

The electronic item encodes printed pp. 84–303 and omits the 1984 volume's front and back matter and printed editorial notes. It therefore supports browsing the selected texts but is not a digital facsimile or complete electronic edition of the book.

extent.description: The XML has 241 flat DIV1 text items. Four conceptual groups are reconstructed from the edition sequence and a scholarly description; 475 P elements, 3,978 LB milestones, and printed page milestones for pp. 84–303 are encoding metrics rather than work counts.

content_parts.label: Signet and related documents
content_parts.summary: Items 1–109: 105 documents presented as Henry V Signet material (1417–1422), followed by four later documents (1434–1455). The edition's departmental and chronological assignments are not uniformly reliable.
notes: The sequence and count are well supported; the category label is inherited from the edition and should not be treated as verified provenance for every item. Benskin reclassifies items 106 and 107 as Privy Seal material.

content_parts.label: Privy Seal papers
content_parts.summary: Items 110–160: documents dated 1408–1455 and grouped by the edition as Privy Seal papers; later critique shows that some boundaries between Signet and Privy Seal material require reassessment.
notes: The sequence and count are well supported; the category label is inherited from the edition and should not be treated as verified provenance for every item. Benskin identifies items 106, 107, 148, 150, and 156 as Privy Seal material on administrative evidence.

content_parts.label: Proceedings in Parliament and Chancery
content_parts.summary: Items 161–232: petitions and other parliamentary or Chancery proceedings dated 1388–1455, including many Ancient Petitions now cataloged in TNA series SC 8.
notes: The sequence and count are well supported; the category label is inherited from the edition and should not be treated as verified provenance for every item.

content_parts.label: Indentures
content_parts.summary: Items 233–241: indentures and related documentary records. The edition/XML headings span 1384–1462; documented corrections to items 240 and 241 yield a currently corrected span of 1384–1455.
notes: The sequence and count are well supported; the category label is inherited from the edition and should not be treated as verified physical form or provenance for every item. Item 241, for example, is a deed poll although its text calls itself an indenture.

evidence.summary: The repository artifact identifies the 1984 source edition, OTA U-1676-B, reports a 1384–1462 span in its inherited headings, and contains 241 numbered document divisions on printed pp. 84–303. The reported upper bound is not the corrected historical date of item 241.

Offline parsing counted 241 DIV1 text items, 241 headings, 475 paragraphs, 3,978 line-break milestones, 216 page-break starts, 217 page-break ends, and 222 NOTE1 elements; page labels span 84–303 with four absent and several out-of-order milestones.

evidence.citation: University of Michigan, Corpus of Middle English Prose and Verse, An anthology of Chancery English
evidence.summary: The public CME record supplies the title, electronic item identity, source-edition statement, and access context.

evidence.citation: John H. Fisher, Malcolm Richardson, and Jane L. Fisher, eds., An Anthology of Chancery English (1984)
evidence.summary: The linked lineage evidence establishes the edition's editors, publication facts, extent, selection boundary, and identifiers.

evidence.citation: María Jesús Pérez Quintero, The Adjectival Group in the English Chancery Documents (2000), p. 138
evidence.summary: The article reports the edition's 241-text total and four edition-defined groups: 109 Signet-related texts, 51 Privy Seal papers, 72 parliamentary/Chancery proceedings, and 9 indentures.

evidence.citation: Michael Benskin, ‘Chancery Standard’ (2004), publisher preview pp. 1–17
evidence.summary: The cached preview documents significant transcription, source-reference, dating, locality, and departmental-classification problems, including reclassification of items 106, 107, 148, 150, and 156; corrections to items 164, 178, 234, 240, and 241; and a print-versus-electronic heading question for item 238. It cautions against treating edition labels as witness facts.

evidence.citation: General Corpus audit of ChancEng references against The National Archives Discovery API
evidence.summary: The linked audit preserves all 231 TNA-cited item mappings, including literal edition references, normalized lookups, current catalog records, digitization status, and exact-versus-parent resolution.

evidence.citation: British Library catalog and IIIF evidence for seven Cotton manuscripts cited by ChancEng
evidence.summary: The linked evidence identifies the seven BL codices containing ten cited item passages and records complete public facsimiles for Cotton MS Vespasian C XII and Cotton MS Vespasian F III.

open_questions.question: Which of the 241 transcriptions, dates, localities, source references, and departmental labels can be confirmed against the physical records?
next_steps: Compare each selected text with a current facsimile or repository reproduction.
Record corrections item by item without silently normalizing the inherited edition.

open_questions.question: What permissions govern redistribution and commercial/POD reuse when U-M labels its electronic text no-copyright but OTA and the CME source folder impose CC BY-NC-SA 3.0 and the 1984 edition remains copyrighted?
next_steps: Seek written permission or clarification from U-M, OTA, and the relevant edition rightsholder before commercial or broad republication.
Keep provider image and archival-reproduction permissions separate from text rights.

open_questions.question: Can the 145 anthology references currently resolved only to a parent catalog piece be mapped to exact modern item-level records, and what do colon-separated or malformed legacy references mean?
next_steps: Consult the printed edition's apparatus and TNA series-level finding aids.
Ask TNA staff about unresolved legacy reference syntax where necessary.

open_questions.question: Why does anthology item 14 identify Cotton MS Vespasian C XII, f. 155, while the current BL catalog describes the matching John Hull order at f. 154r and the IIIF canvas labels the matching image f. 155r?
next_steps: Ask the British Library to reconcile historical and current foliation.
Retain both reported locators in the lineage record.

notes: Edition-derived group names are discovery aids, not verified statements that every source was created by the named administrative office.
The XML's missing and out-of-order printed-page milestones are encoding anomalies; they are not evidence that the corresponding edition pages or manuscript leaves are absent.
Detailed item-level archival and facsimile access is maintained in the linked lineage manifest rather than flattened into this descriptive record.
